annotations.text: I suspect that transient tachypnea of the newborn prompted the initial x-ray film of the chest, which demonstrated the mass. Therefore, we are probably dealing with an incidental finding.
The discovery at birth of a mass that proved to be relatively quiescent for the first 17 weeks of life leads me to consider a differential diagnosis of neoplastic and congenital lesions.
If there had been air it would have been easy to detect it on the CT scan.

the cleavage plane between the paraspinal tissues and the mass, the absence of spinal involvement, the absence of calcification, and the lack of evidence of distortion of adjacent structures such as the ribs, blood vessels, and airways make the diagnosis of a neural-crest tumor unlikely in this case from a radiographic standpoint.
Neuroblastomas, sarcomas, teratomas, and leukemia are those most frequently described.
I agree with them, although I would have needed the added comfort provided by a negative assay of urine for catecholamines.
About 90 percent of patients with thoracic neural-crest tumors have elevated levels of dopa, dopamine, norepinephrine, normetanephrine, vanilmandelic acid (VMA), and homovanillic acid (HVA).
Occasional reports of a systemic blood supply to this malformation can cause confusion with the next consideration, pulmonary sequestration.
